title: practical english usage - Part 39

present evidence - to say that a future action or event is on the way, or starting Another use of the going-to structure is to predict the future on the basis of 3  things     that    are   on   the   way:   She's     going       to   have      a  baby.

I'm going to stop him  reading my emails if it's the last thing I do.

I'm going to keep asking her out until she says 'Yes'.

When are you going to get your hair cut?

Jack says he's going to phone this evening.

We're going to get ɑ  new car soon.

has already been made.

style. This structure often emphasises the idea of intention, or a decision that We use be going to + infinitive to talk about plans, especially in an informal 2 plans:   We're      going       to   get    a  new     car.

is often about future actions and events of this kind).

is very common in an informal style, especially in speech (because conversation decided, or it is starting to happen, or we  can see it coming now. The  structure that something in the future is going to happen, it is usually already planned or to talk about future actions and events that have some present reality. If we  say This structure is really a present tense (the present progressive of go). We   use it 1  a  present      tense 35 going       to grammar •  36   present progressive for future looking over the river.) not Their new house is going to look over the river. permanent states. Compare: And the present progressive is used for actions and events, but not usually for better soon.) not I can see that things are going to get better soon. (     .. .things are getting not It's going to snow before long. (     It's snowing before long.) control.

is not generally used to make predictions about events that are outside people’ s Because the present progressive is used especially for personal arrangements, it Who's going to cook lunch? (asking for a decision) - Who's cooking lunch? (asking what has been arranged) NOT   I’m  really telling him  ...) I'm really going to tell him  what I think of him. (emphasis on intention: - I'm seeing Jake tonight. (emphasis on arrangement) Compare: talking not about fixed arrangements, but about intentions and decisions.

But there are some differences. For example, we  prefer going to ... when we  are I'm washing / going to wash my hair this evening.

In many cases, both structures can be used to express the same idea.

2 present      progressive         and   going       to  ...: differences not What are you doing this evening? (     What do you do this evening?) (but ►    37).

Note that the simple present is not often used to talk about the future Get your coat on! I'm taking you down to the doctor!

I'm Just going next door. Back in a minute.

Are you coming to the pub?

actions which  are just starting.

We   often use the present progressive with verbs of movement, to talk about My car's going in for repairs next week.

What are we having for dinner?

We're touring Mexico next summer.

Did you know I'm getting a new job?

I'm seeing Larry on Saturday.

'What are you doing this evening?’ 'I'm washing my hair.' fixed plans, when the time and place have been decided.

present reality. It is most common in discussions of personal arrangements and We use the present progressive for future actions and events that have some 1  present      reality:    I'm  washing         my    hair     this    evening.

In other cases, we  do not usually use the simple present to talk about the future.
